title: Middara - Take a Look
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=C9FJCiew0s8

Daniel (@dungeondive) opens his (paid, late-backer) copy of Middara: Unintentional Malum — Act 1, and is upfront that this is a take-a-look, not a review: the game is so massive it'll take months of sessions to judge. What's clear immediately is the ambition. Succubus Publishing, a young studio on its first game, approaches it like an indie video-game developer — explicitly chasing the feel of tactical-action JRPGs like Final Fantasy Tactics and Vandal Hearts, complete with an anime art style that will instantly divide viewers, and even an Abraxas mini-game.

This is a narrative-driven game in the truest sense: a spiral-bound 64-page rulebook he praises as well-organized, and a nearly 500-page adventure book — the biggest quest book he's ever seen — carrying a tightly-scripted, branching story where characters join, leave, and may die. He walks through the heavy promo/stretch box, ~40 solid minis, a black push-your-luck "empowerment" die he loves, and a vast pool of double-sided equipment cards with creative builds. His one real worry: no packing insert for a long voyage from China (a tentacle already snapped, books scuffed). A passion project, clearly — verdict reserved for the play.

----

Middara wears its JRPG and anime influences on its sleeve, right down to a 500-page scripted story. Does a board game leaning that hard into video-game DNA excite you, or do you want the tabletop to be its own thing?
